preFix.title: Throwing a cylindrical candle holder
preFix.subtitle: A narrow upright cylinder sized for a standard pillar candle
preFix.excerpt: A thrown stoneware candle holder with a 50 mm diameter well and walls at least 6 mm thick for thermal safety. The compressed base and fully glazed interior and exterior make it suitable for regular use with a pillar candle. This is a practical first project once you can centre and pull a basic cylinder.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

This candle holder is a narrow upright cylinder thrown in smooth stoneware, sized to accept a standard 50 mm diameter pillar candle. The well is approximately 100 mm deep and the walls are a minimum of 6 mm thick to buffer the heat from a burning candle. A firmly compressed base adds further thermal protection. The piece requires both a wheel and a kiln, and is glazed inside and out at cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C) so it is safe for regular use.
Preparation

Wedge 500 g of smooth stoneware thoroughly to remove air pockets. The 50 mm internal diameter is narrower than a standard mug, so centring and opening precisely matters more here than on wider forms. The internal dimension is best checked with a ruler rather than by eye. Throw onto a bat so you can lift the finished piece off the wheel without distortion.

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: enough to dip or brush inside and out

content.text: Throwing the body
Secure the bat on the wheel head. Centre the 500 g ball of clay at medium speed. Once the clay runs without wobble, reduce speed to a comfortable pulling pace.

Open the centre with your dominant thumb, pressing straight down to leave a base approximately 10 mm thick. Check the base depth by gently pressing a needle tool until you feel resistance, then measuring the depth against the tool with your finger.
Widen the floor of the opening until the internal diameter reaches 50 mm. Use your ruler or needle tool held horizontally across the opening to confirm the measurement before pulling the walls.
Compress the base firmly with the flat of two fingers, working from the centre outward in overlapping circles. A well-compressed base is less likely to crack during drying or firing.

Pull the walls upward in three or four steady passes, keeping the inside hand slightly higher than the outside. Each pull should thin the wall by roughly the same amount. Target a wall thickness of 6 to 8 mm throughout: thick enough for thermal safety, thin enough to throw without collapsing.
Keep the cylinder tall and straight rather than letting it belly outward. If the form flares, gently collar inward with both hands cupped around the exterior and increase wheel speed slightly.

Once the cylinder reaches approximately 120 mm tall (the 100 mm well depth plus a small amount for trimming), smooth the rim with a damp chamois. Use the wooden rib on the outside wall to compress and refine the surface.

Remove excess water from the interior with a small sponge on a stick: standing water left inside a tall narrow cylinder will weaken the base and cause the walls to slump. Cut the piece off the bat with a wire cutter and leave the bat in place to dry undisturbed overnight.
Trimming
When the piece has reached the
leather-hard
stage (firm but still cool and slightly damp to the touch), centre it upside down on the wheel head and secure with a ring of small clay pads.
Trim the base using the loop tool to create a low
foot ring
approximately 4 mm wide. Keep the foot ring narrow: a wide ring traps heat directly under the pot. Leave at least 8 mm of clay in the base even after trimming, to maintain the thermal buffer.
Lightly smooth the foot ring and the trimmed base area with a damp sponge. Remove any sharp edges from the rim. Return the piece right-side up and leave to dry completely.
Drying and bisque firing
Cover the piece loosely with thin plastic for the first 24 hours to slow drying and reduce the risk of rim cracking on the narrow cylinder. Remove the plastic and allow the piece to reach the
bone-dry
stage over the following two to three days. The piece is now
greenware
and ready for bisque firing. Load in the kiln and fire to
cone
06 (approximately 1000 degrees C). The piece emerges as
bisque
: hard, porous, and ready for glazing.
Glazing
Wipe the bisqueware with a barely damp sponge to remove any dust. Leave to dry for ten minutes before applying glaze.

Glaze the inside first. Pour or dip the pre-mixed mid-fire glaze into the well, swirl to coat the interior evenly, then tip it back into the glaze bucket. Allow the interior glaze to dry for five minutes.

Dip the exterior. Hold the piece by the foot ring and lower the exterior into the glaze to within approximately 5 mm of the foot ring. Hold for three seconds, then lift cleanly. Leave the foot ring and the bottom 5 mm of the base unglazed: glaze in contact with the kiln shelf fuses to it on firing.
Once dry, wax the foot ring with kiln wax if you have it, or clean any accidental drips from the foot ring with a damp sponge before loading the kiln.
Glaze firing

Place the piece on a clean kiln shelf with the foot ring down. Fire to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C) following the glaze manufacturer's recommended schedule. Allow the kiln to cool naturally. Do not open the kiln lid until the interior temperature is below 80 degrees C. The glaze will have melted and fused to the clay body, producing a watertight surface inside and out.
Care

The candle holder is food-safe as long as the glaze is rated food-safe by the supplier and is fully melted across the interior. Use it with pillar candles only, not tealights in metal cups. Never leave a burning candle unattended. When the candle burns down to within 20 mm of the base, replace it to avoid heat concentrating at the base for extended periods. Wipe the interior with a damp cloth to remove wax residue between uses.
Variations
Taller lantern form: extend the pull to 160 mm and add a series of small pierced holes near the top using a needle tool at leather-hard, so candlelight glows through.
Textured exterior: press a wooden stamp or fabric into the leather-hard wall before trimming to add surface interest.
Set of three: throw the same form at 300 g, 400 g, and 500 g of clay for a graduated trio that nest loosely for storage.

rows.fix: Use less water, allow the clay to stiffen slightly between pulls, and keep each pull shorter rather than trying to thin the wall in one pass
rows.cause: Clay too wet or walls too thin before the final pull
rows.problem: Cylinder walls collapse inward during pulling

rows.fix: Cover the piece loosely with thin plastic for the first 24 to 2 days to slow evaporation evenly
rows.cause: Uneven drying: the rim dries faster than the base in the sheltered interior of the narrow cylinder

rows.fix: Wipe the bisqueware thoroughly before glazing; apply glaze in two thin coats rather than one thick coat
rows.cause: Glaze applied too thickly, or dust or grease on the bisque surface prevented adhesion
rows.problem: Glaze crawls off the interior wall after firing

rows.fix: Clean the foot ring before loading; apply kiln wash to the shelf; leave a wider bare margin at the base
rows.cause: Glaze ran down to the foot ring during firing
rows.problem: Piece sticks to the kiln shelf
